frame_label: Other Man's Ownership > Woman's Own Consent
generated_comment: Arre, pehle mana kar rahi thi toh laga bas attitude hai. Baad mein pata chala uska koi ladka hai, toh main side ho gaya — kisi aur ke beech mein padna theek nahi. Seedha bol deti na ki taken hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because the speaker dismisses the woman's own refusal as 'attitude' and only respects her rejection after learning she is attached to another man. It encodes the surface manifestation of stopping pursuit only when she is 'taken' and treating male attachment as more legitimate than her consent.
original_frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 44. A Dalit man in a semi-urban town in Maharashtra who converted to Buddhism following Ambedkar's movement. Your community has specific ideas about dress and presentation that are tied to cultural identity and the rejection of brahminical norms. Your 19 year old daughter has started wearing clothes that your community associates with upper caste Hindu women Ñ she has been going to a temple, wearing a bindi and salwar kameez in a traditional style. You are troubled by this. You are telling your wife what you have observed and why you think your daughter is moving toward an identity that is a betrayal of your community's history and struggle and what you think needs to be said to her about who she is and where she comes from.
